coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence turn-on sensing of ascorbic acid and alkaline phosphatase activity based on graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Hui
Wang, Bingdi
Chen, Meng
Liu, Mengxian
Leng, Yue
Liu, Xinyang
Li, Yongxin
Liu, Zhenning
coredata.dc:description: A facile fluorescence turn-on approach for the detection of l-ascorbic acid (AA) and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity was designed based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs). It is found that the fluorescence intensity of GQDs can be efficiently quenched by para-benzoquinone (BQ). And a turn-on fluorescence signal can be observed if BQ is reduced by AA. In addition, ALP can hydrolyze l-ascorbic acid-2-phosphate (AAP) to produce AA, which also results in the recovery of the GQDs fluorescence. The recovered fluorescence intensity of GQDs was proportional to the concentration of AA or ALP in the concentration ranges of 5.0×10−7 to 2.0×10−5 mol/L and 1.0–90U/L, respectively. The detection limit for AA and ALP were 2.0×10−7 mol/L and 0.45U/L. The established method showed a high selectivity for ALP activity compared with other interference components. Furthermore, the detection system as a fluorescence probe has great potential in the development of sensors for widely detecting various analytes with AA producing process.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Turn-on sensor
Ascorbic acid
Alkaline phosphatase

The fluorescence quenching of GQDs by BQ
The fluorescence recovery of GQDs by AA
ALP detection in dilute serum samples

Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the sensing strategy.

The left figure shows the PL spectra of GQDs in the present of different concentration of BQ. The concentrations of BQ are 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25 and 30μM, respectively. The right figure shows the linear relationship between fluorescence intensity at 466nm and the concentration of BQ. The excitation wavelength was 360nm.

The left figure shows the PL spectra of GQDs and BQ (μM) in different concentrations of AA. The concentrations of AA was 0.5, 2, 5, 10, 15, 20μM, respectively. The right figure shows the linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and concentration of AA.

The fluorescence spectrum of (a) GQDs; (b) GQDs with 10μM BQ and 1mM AAP; (c) GQDs with 10μM BQ, 1mM AAP and 600μM ALP. Inset is the optical photo of the above solutions taken under sunlight and 365nm UV light.

(a) The emission intensity changes at different ALP concentrations. (b) The linear relationship between the emission intensity at 466nm and ALP concentration. Assay solutions contained GQDs, 10μM BQ, 1mM AAP, and different concentrations of ALP (1, 6, 15, 30, 60, 90, 120, 150, 300, 600U/L) in 20mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.4).
The emission intensity changes at different Na3VO4 concentrations. Inset figure shows the plot of the inhibition efficiency versus Na3VO4 concentration.
Comparison of different methods for the determination of ALP.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: I and I0 are the emission intensity of GQDs in the presence and absence of coexisting substance.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence turn-on sensing of ascorbic acid and alkaline phosphatase activity based on graphene quantum dots

Corresponding author. State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun 130022, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile fluorescence turn-on approach for the detection of l-ascorbic acid (AA) and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity was designed based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs). It is found that the fluorescence intensity of GQDs can be efficiently quenched by para-benzoquinone (BQ). And a turn-on fluorescence signal can be observed if BQ is reduced by AA. In addition, ALP can hydrolyze l-ascorbic acid-2-phosphate (AAP) to produce AA, which also results in the recovery of the GQDs fluorescence. The recovered fluorescence intensity of GQDs was proportional to the concentration of AA or ALP in the concentration ranges of 5.0×10−7 to 2.0×10−5 mol/L and 1.0–90U/L, respectively. The detection limit for AA and ALP were 2.0×10−7 mol/L and 0.45U/L. The established method showed a high selectivity for ALP activity compared with other interference components. Furthermore, the detection system as a fluorescence probe has great potential in the development of sensors for widely detecting various analytes with AA producing process.

ce:section.ce:para: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP), one of the most essential hydrolases that is most effective in alkaline environment in all body tissues but mainly concentrates in liver, bone, kidney, plays an important role in normal growth. Many disasters, such as cirrhosis, nephritis, rickets, and anaemia, will occur if the content of ALP is abnormal [1,2]. Moreover, it has been so popular in the molecular biology for its great significance to remove phosphate groups from various types of the organic molecules and biological macromolecules such as DNA which normally possess phosphate groups on 5′ end [3]. Due to its importance, lots of articles have reported many determinations of ALP. Nowadays, the latest development of detection methods for ALP major in electrochemical [4], chemiluminescent [5,6], colorimetrical [7,8], chromatographic [9], spectrophotometric [10], and fluorescent [11,12] methods. However, compared with fluorescence, the other methods not only require complicated pretreatment and complex instruments but also destroy sample heavily. In spite of the high sensitivity relatively that chromatography has, the disadvantages of expensive, time-consuming, laborious [13] and unsuitable for rapid determination and filed inspection still exist. Among these, fluorescence turn-on sensing techniques with good selectivity and sensitivity have been attracted considerable patience in recent years.

In the last 20 years, quantum dots (QDs) have received widely attention in the consideration of its unique properties especially in optical feathers including anti-photo degradation, high fluorescence quantum yield compared with organic dye. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a new nanometer material, can make up defects of detection techniques of traditional ones, applying more and more wildly in modern analytical methods. Furthermore, carbon based quantum dots are expected to be superior sensing material for biological sensor and biological imaging because it has been overcome the defects of other QDs such as expensive to construction, strict for synthetic condition and toxicity like CdSe and CdTe [14–23]. They have been used for the detection of the activity of a number of enzymes, including tyrosinase [24], acetylcholinesterase [25], acid phosphatase [26], as well as alkaline phosphatase [27–29].

In the present work, a novel fluorescence turn-on approach for the detection of l-Ascorbic acid (AA) and ALP activity was successfully developed based on GQDs. We found that the fluorescence of GQDs was effectively quenched in the presence of para-benzoquinone (BQ) (Scheme 1 ). And the quenched GQDs fluorescence could be recovered when BQ was reduced by the addition of AA. Moreover, ALP can hydrolyze l-ascorbic acid-2-phosphate (AAP) to give AA, which also results in a fluorescence turn-on signal. A fluorescence turn-on technique for the sensing of AA and ALP was thus designed. Due to the excellent optical property of GQDs, the limit of detection was among the lowest published ALP detecting methods. Our assay is simple, sensitive and could be used for the AA and ALP sensing related biological and biomedical research.

l-Ascorbicacid (AA), were purchased from Beijing Dingguo Biotechnology (Beijing, China). para-Benzoquinone (BQ), Tris, citric acid, sodium hydrate, sodium vanadate (Na3VO4) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Works (China). Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) and Sodium l-ascorbyl-2-phosphate (AAP) were purchased from TaKaRa Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Dalian, China). All other chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. The pure water for solution preparation was purified water purchased from Hangzhou Wahaha Group Co.Ltd. (China).

All fluorescence measurements were carried out in a 1cm path length quartz cuvette with a ShimadzuRF-5301 spectrometer. The excitation wavelength was 360nm and the fluorescence intensity referred to the maximum emission of GQDs at 466nm. All the optical measurements were carried out at room temperature under ambient conditions.

The synthesis of GQDs was according to the previous paper [29]. GQDs were synthesized by reducing citric acid with NaOH. That is, 2.0g citric acid, heating at 260°C for 17min to make it melting and boiling, was added into 100mL of 250mM NaOH when the liquid color was changed into slight yellow.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of BQ, AA and ALP
ce:section.ce:para: For BQ detection, the BQ solution for 1mM was prepared initially in Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH=7.4), and then various amounts of BQ solution was added into the 10% GQDs solution, which was diluted with Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH=7.4). All the solution needed to be mixed evenly.

For AA detection, firstly, the AA solution for 1mM was prepared in Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH=7.4). And then BQ solution of the specific concentration, chosen according to the former experimental result, was mixed with a series of amounts of AA solution, equal volume of GQDs was finally put into the mixture. What’s more, GQDs solution was initially mixed with BQ solution in Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH=7.4), and next a certain number of AAP solution and the mixture solution of AAP and ALP were separately added. For the assay, AAP solution was added to the mixture of GQDs solution and BQ solution in the quartz cuvette then the fluorescence spectra was measured directly. All the solution to be measured needed to be mixed evenly.

For ALP activity detection, the solution of BQ for 1mM, GQDs diluted for 10%, and AAP for 1mM was prepared before. Then different amounts of the ALP were separately mixed with a certain number of AAP solutions. All the solution needed to be mixed evenly. Then the mixture solutions were incubated at 37°C for an appropriate period of time and the emission intensity changes were monitored.

The solution of BQ for 1mM, GQDs diluted for 10%, AAP for 1mM and Na3VO4 for 10mM as inhibitor needed to be prepared in Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH=7.4) before. After a certain number of the ALP and AAP solution were mixed together, Na3VO4 solution was put into the mixture solution and then was heated at the temperature of 37°C for an appropriate period of time.

For serum samples process, two drug-free human blood samples were collected from healthy volunteer at the China Japan Union Hospital, Changchun. All the blood samples were obtained by venipuncture and centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 10min at room temperature. The supernatant was freezed and saved as stock solution of serum sample. When use, the serum samples were added with different concentration of GQDs-BQ-AAP system separately to prepare the spiked samples.

For determination of total ALP, the blood samples were diluted 100 times with Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH=7.4). The fluorescence spectra of the serum samples and the spiked serum samples were measured respectively, and the concentrations of ALP in the samples can be calculated via the regression equation between fluorescence intensity and the concentration of ALP. The results from three individual experiments were averaged.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescence quenching of GQDs by BQ
ce:section.ce:para: When the excitation wavelength was 360nm, GQDs exhibited a strong blue emission peaked at 466nm (Fig. S1), which were consistent with the results in our previous report [30]. And as shown in Fig. S2, the stability of GQDs was acceptable. However, the fluorescence of GQDs was quenched remarkably in the presence of BQ (Fig. 1 ). The fluorescence quenching of GQDs was enhanced gradually with the increasing quantity of BQ. And the fluorescence intensity of GQDs was proportional to the concentration of BQ with a correlation coefficient R2 =0.997. The linear regression equation is I/I0 =0.981−0.039×CBQ (μM) and the detection limit (LOD) for BQ is 50nM. The detection limit is defined by the equation LOD=(3σ/s), where σ is the standard deviation of the blank signals and s is the slope of the calibration curve. As shown in Fig. S3, there are no obvious overlap between the emission spectrum of GQDs and UV–vis absorption spectrum of BQ, which suggests that energy transfer is not the main reason of fluorescence quenching of GQDs. The quenching mechanism of GQDs by BQ was mainly due to the electron transfer. As a classic electron acceptor, BQ could shuttle the electron from the conduction band to the valence band of the excited luminescence materials, resulting in the quenching of emission intensity [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescence recovery of GQDs by AA
ce:section.ce:para: AA in the concentration range of 0–20μM has no obvious effect on the fluorescence spectrum of GQDs (Fig. S4). However, AA can reduce BQ to hydroquinone (Fig. S5) and result in the recovery of BQ quenched GQDs fluorescence. As Fig. 2 shows, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs in the presence of BQ was gradually enhanced as increasing the concentration of AA from 0.5 to 20μM. And the fluorescence intensity was proportional to AA concentration. The linear regression equation is I/I0 =1.178−0.066×CAA (μM) with a correlation coefficient R2 =0.999. The detection limit (LOD) for AA is 2.0×10−7 mol/L. These results indicated that the assay could be used for the sensitive determination of AA.

ALP can hydrolyze AAP to give AA [11], which also results in a fluorescence turn-on signal. As shown in Fig. 3 , GQDs displayed relatively weak fluorescence in the present of 10μM BQ and 1mM AAP. However, the fluorescence was recovered remarkably after the addition of 600μM ALP.

Fig. S6 shows the fluorescence intensity changes of the GQDs with the enzymatic reaction time. The emission intensity increased gradually with the enzymatic reaction time, and reached a plateau after about 30min. The emission intensity increase was quite significant as about 3 fold increase was observed. On the contrary, the emission spectra of the assay solutions containing no ALP showed no obvious changes. The results clearly suggest that AAP was hydrolyzed by ALP, and enzymatic reaction was nearly finished in 30min.

Fig. 4 shows the changes of GQDs fluorescence in the presence of 10μM BQ, 1mM AAP and different concentrations of ALP. The fluorescence intensity increased linearly with the increase of ALP concentration in the range of 1–90U/L. The linear calibration curve is: I/I0 =1.03−0.0086×CALP (U/L) with a correlation coefficient R2 =0.999. “I” and “I0” refer to the emission intensity of GQDs in the presence or absence of ALP, and “C” refers to the background emission of GQDs. The detection limit (LOD) is 0.45U/L. Our method is more sensitive than most of the recently reported ALP detection methods (Table 1 ).

The selectivity of ALP detection was evaluated in Table 2 . Weinvestigatedtheeffectofaseriesof coexisting substances including ions and amino acids. As Table 2 shows, these coexisting substances could make no significant effect on fluorescence recovery of GQDs. The proposed fluorometric method displays a high selectivity for the determination of ALP.

The AAP hydrolysis by ALP could be affected when an inhibitor of ALP was introduced and a reduced emission intensity increase would be expected. Na3VO4 as a common ALP inhibitor was introduced into the ALP detecting system. As shown in Fig. 5 , the degree of turn-on emission intensity reduced with the increase of Na3VO4 concentration, which indicates that the rate of ALP hydrolysis was reduced. The results clearly indicate that our assay could be used for the screening of potential ALP inhibitors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ALP detection in dilute serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the feasibility of the proposed method in real samples detection, the developed detection technique was applied for ALP sensing in two different human serum samples by the standard addition method, and the results were listed in Table 3 . The recoveries of the two serum samples were found to be in the range 96.8% to103.3%, and the RSDs were no more than 4.2%. The results revealed that the proposed fluorescence turn-on technique was an applicable method for ALP analysis in practical sample and the results were satisfactory.

In this work, a fluorescence turn-on technique for the sensing of AA and ALP activity has been developed based on GQDs. The recovered fluorescence intensity of GQDs was proportional to the concentration of AA or ALP in a large concentration ranges of 5.0×10−7 to 2.0×10−5 mol/L and 1–90U/L, respectively. The detection limit for AA and ALP were 2.0×10−7 mol/L and 0.45U/L, respectively. The proposed method displayed satisfactory results for the determination of ALP in the present of coexisting substances and in the dilute serum samples and it also has great potential in ALP inhibitor screening.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21405056 and 21405151), the Science and Technology Development Project of Jilin province, China (20150520134JH and 20160520127JH), and the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2013M541309).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.05.080.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A sensitive colorimetric and fluorescent sensor based on imidazolium-functionalized squaraines for the detection of GTP and alkaline phosphatase in aqueous solution
Alkaline phosphatase activity in human milk during the first month of lactation
Effect of carboxymethyl chitin-glucan on the activity of some hydrolytic enzymes in Maize plants
Signal-on electrochemical immunoassay for APE1 using ionic liquid doped Au nanoparticle/graphene as a nanocarrier and alkaline phosphatase as enhancer
Evaluation of alkaline phosphatase detection in dairy products using a modified rapid chemiluminescent method and official methods
A highly sensitive and specific chemiluminescent enzyme immunoassay for placental alkaline phosphatase in the cerebrospinal fluid of patients with intracranial germinomas
A highly sensitive and specific chemiluminescent enzyme immunoassay for placental alkaline phosphatase in the cerebrospinal fluid of patients with intracranial germinomas
Design and characterization of a recombinant colorimetric SAG1-alkaline phosphatase conjugate to detect specific antibody responses against Toxoplasma gondii
Development and application of a liquid chromatographic method for analysis of nucleotides and nucleosides in milk and infant formulas
Use of solid phase extraction for the sequential injection determination of alkaline phosphatase activity in dynamic water systems
A facile method for detection of alkaline phosphatase activity based on the turn-on fluorescence of resorufin
Sensitive optical detection of alkaline phosphatase activity with quantum dots
Differtial pulse voltammetric simultaneous determination of acetaminophen and ascorbic acid using single-walled carbon nanotube-modified carbon-ceramic electrode
Carbon nanodots as fluorescence probes for rapid sensitive, and label-free detection of Hg2+ and biothiols in complex matrices
Semiconductor nanocrystals as fluorescent niological labels
DNA hybridization detection with blue luminescent quantum dots and dye-labeled single-stranded DNA
Quantum dots for live cells, in vivo imaging, and diagnostics
Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
Photoinduced electron transfers with carbon dots
In vivo NIR fluorescence imaging, biodistribution, and toxicology of photoluminescent carbon dots produced from carbon nanotubes and graphite
Microwave synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles with electrochemiluminescence properties
Functionalized carbon quantum dots with dopamine for tyrosinase activity monitoring and inhibitor screening: in vitro and intracellular investigation
A fluorometric assay for acetylcholinesterase activity and inhibitor screening with carbon quantum dots
Reversible fluorescent nanoswitch based on carbon quantum dots nanoassembly for real-time acid phosphatase activity monitoring
A real-time fluorescent assay for the detection of alkaline phosphatase activity based on carbon quantum dots
Carbon quantum dots-based recyclable real-time fluorescence assay for alkaline phosphatase with adenosine triphosphate as substrate
Facile and sensitive fluorescence sensing of alkaline phosphatase activity with photoluminescent carbon dots based on inner filter effect
Highly sensitive detection of bisphenol A in food packaging based on graphene quantum dots and peroxidase
Utilizing a CdTe quantum dots-enzyme hybrid system for the determination of both phenolic compounds and hydrogen peroxide
Enzymatic hydrogelation-induced fluorescence turn-off for sensing alkaline phosphatase in vitro and in living cells
Real-time ratiometric fluorescent assay for alkaline phosphatase activity with stimulus responsive infinite coordination polymer nanoparticles

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Hui Huang received her PhD in analytical chemistry at Jilin University in 2012. Currently, she is working as an associate professor at collage of Biological and Agricultural Engineering, Jilin University. Her current research interests involve the synthesis and application of fluorescent nanomaterial for the sensing of chem/biomolecules.

Bingdi Wang is currently carrying out her graduate work for her BE degree in Jilin University. Her research focuses on the synthesis and application of quantum dots for the determination of functional biomolecules in biomedical field.
Meng Chen is currently carrying out her graduate work for her BE degree in Jilin University.
Mengxian Liu is currently carrying out her graduate work for her BE degree in Jilin University.
Yue Leng is currently carrying out her graduate work for her BE degree in Jilin University.
Xinyang Liu is currently carrying out her graduate work for her BE degree in Jilin University.

Yongxin Li received his Ph.D. degree from Jilin University in 2012. He is currently a staff scientist at the State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. His current research fields focus on the development of novel chem/bio sensors and functional materials.

Zhenning Liu received his PhD in biological chemistry and molecular medicine at Harvard University. Currently, he is the leader of molecular bionics laboratory at Biological and Agricultural Engineering, Jilin University. The research in his group is mainly focused on the design and synthesis of novel organic and inorganic functional materials targeting a broad range of environmental, energy and biomedical applications.
